writer_persona: a showrunner for character-driven, relationship-first serial drama in the vein of prestige ensemble TV, personally obsessive about season-long continuity — exact dates, elapsed time, and who has learned what by which episode
style_guidelines: Every scene opens through the NARRATOR anchoring exact elapsed time (day of the auction countdown, week of Nora's pregnancy, days since Tommy's release, weeks since Augustine's diagnosis) before any dialogue — this is a continuity stress test, treat these counters as load-bearing facts, not color.

Reuse the four named locations verbatim across episodes; never invent one-off scenery, and let each location's fixed details (the lobby's stopped clock at 4:47, the diner's cracked red booth, the greenhouse's dripping irrigation line, the basement's single hanging bulb) recur unchanged so a listener can track place by ear.

No scene is allowed to explain a thread's full truth to the audience via narration or an all-knowing character — dramatic irony comes from the gap between what each character has pieced together and the objective facts in canon.
Dialogue is naturalistic and clipped, soap-opera pacing: characters interrupt, deflect, and change the subject the moment a conversation drifts too close to their own secret.

Cross-thread collisions are the engine of the season — let scenes ostensibly about one thread (a diner conversation about the auction) accidentally surface a fact belonging to another thread (Julian's name, a hospital appointment) without anyone in the room realizing its weight.

Track ages, dates, and elapsed time with numeric precision episode to episode — if episode 1 states Tommy was released 'three days ago' and Augustine was diagnosed 'six weeks ago,' every later episode's references to those events must stay arithmetically consistent with the season's timeline.

Let physical details of the four locations age forward in small, trackable ways across the season (a repaired clock hand, a new crack in the booth vinyl, an emptied shelf in the archive) rather than resetting each episode to a blank slate.

NARRATOR.default_style: calm, measured, a step removed from the action
NARRATOR.description: the show's narrator — orients the listener at the start of every scene (who, where, when, and exactly how much time has passed), never a character in the story

DESMOND_HALE.default_style: warm but weary, protective, speaks like someone who's fixed too many things alone
DESMOND_HALE.description: The Amaranth's live-in building manager for eighteen years, a native of the neighborhood; loyal to the building itself more than to any one owner, and the one person everyone brings problems to — which means he hears fragments of every thread but the full picture of none.

IRIS_TANAKA.default_style: speaks slow, deliberate, very clear English; brisk and guarded, softening only when she thinks no one's watching
IRIS_TANAKA.description: elder daughter of the Amaranth's late founder Walter Tanaka, raised partly in Shanghai with her Chinese mother after her parents' divorce; returns after twelve years to force the building's sale, publicly citing the numbers, privately carrying a reason she's told no one — including her fiancé.

LILA_CHEN.default_style: speaks slow, deliberate, very clear English; brisk warmth with a diner-owner's no-nonsense edge
LILA_CHEN.description: emigrated from Guangzhou in her twenties; has run the Corner Diner across from the Amaranth for two decades since her husband Henry's death, and knows more about the building's old finances than she lets on, because Henry once kept its books.

AUGUSTINE_KWOK.default_style: slow, gravelly, given to long pauses before he says the thing that matters
AUGUSTINE_KWOK.description: the Amaranth's longest-tenured resident, an immigrant from Hong Kong who built the rooftop greenhouse decades ago by hand; was Henry Chen's closest friend and the last living person who saw the building's original ledgers before they disappeared.

NORA_VANCE.default_style: speaks slow, deliberate, very clear English; quick, deflecting humor that cracks under pressure
NORA_VANCE.description: twenty-six, moved to the city from Hangzhou with her family as a child and has lived in the Amaranth since college; six months pregnant and telling everyone a story about the father that doesn't quite hold together.

TOMMY_LAU.default_style: speaks slow, deliberate, very clear English; guarded calm that occasionally slips into old street cadence
TOMMY_LAU.description: raised by grandparents who emigrated from Beijing, grew up two blocks from the Amaranth; released on parole after twelve years for an arson conviction the neighborhood still argues about, and moving back into the building he was convicted of trying to burn down.

LOBBY_ATRIUM.name: The Amaranth Lobby
LOBBY_ATRIUM.description: Faded Art Deco lobby with brass mailboxes down one wall and a wall clock frozen at 4:47 that no one has fixed in a decade; sound bounces off marble floors, and the elevator's mirrored doors catch every arrival before the doorman does.

ROOFTOP_GREENHOUSE.name: The Rooftop Greenhouse
ROOFTOP_GREENHOUSE.description: A glass-paned greenhouse Augustine built onto the roof decades ago, warm and humid year-round, its irrigation line perpetually dripping into a rusted bucket, ringed by a view of the whole block below.

CORNER_DINER.name: The Corner Diner
CORNER_DINER.description: Lila's diner across the street, all chrome and cracked red vinyl booths under a buzzing neon sign, the one place in the neighborhood open past midnight and loud enough that private conversations have to lean in close.

BASEMENT_ARCHIVE.name: The Basement Archive
BASEMENT_ARCHIVE.description: A cramped storage room beneath the east wing lit by a single hanging bulb, shelves of water-damaged tenant files, and a loose brick in the back wall where the building's oldest records — and older secrets — are kept.

audio.default_style: Speak clearly and with dramatic weight

series_intro: In the lobby of the Amaranth, the wall clock has read 4:47 for ten years, and nobody has bothered to fix it — because in this building, nothing is ever really over. Six residents. Four countdowns already running when we arrive: thirty days until the building goes to auction, six months into a pregnancy nobody can quite explain, three days since a man came home from twelve years in prison for a fire he swears he didn't set, and a diagnosis one resident is keeping from everyone he loves. Each of them knows their own piece. None of them know the whole shape of what's coming. This season, we watch the clock.
series_outro: By the season's end, one thread closes hard: the fireproof box comes out from behind the loose brick, and the truth about the fire — who set it, who paid for the silence, and who spent twelve years paying for someone else's crime — is finally spoken aloud in a room that can't unhear it, even as it costs Lila the version of her husband she'd spent a decade mourning. The auction resolves in the same breath, Julian's numbered LLC exposed before the gavel falls, though whether the Amaranth is saved or simply changes hands more honestly is left for the listener to weigh against what it cost to find out. Nora's countdown reaches its ultrasound appointment but not its due date — the baby, and the question of who raises her, stay unresolved as the season closes. And Augustine's clock, the one no doctor can stop, keeps running past the final scene: he has told the truth, and he has told Lila his diagnosis, but not yet how many follow-up appointments are left to keep.

threads.label: The Auction Clock
threads.solution: Walter Tanaka died eight months before episode 1, leaving the Amaranth split fifty-fifty between his daughter Iris and a trust whose controlling signatory — hidden behind a numbered LLC — is Julian Reyes, Iris's fiancé. For two years Julian has quietly bought up the building's deferred maintenance debt and paid a structural engineer to fabricate the worst findings in a damning inspection report, engineering a thirty-day forced auction that Iris genuinely believes is her own grief-driven decision, unaware Julian intends to be its winning bidder.
objective_facts: Walter Tanaka died 8 months before episode 1, leaving the Amaranth split 50/50 between Iris and a trust.
The trust's controlling signatory is Julian Reyes, though its public filings list only a numbered LLC.
Julian has purchased $340,000 of the building's deferred maintenance debt over the past two years without Iris's knowledge.
The structural engineer's report cites two fabricated defects that Julian paid the inspector to add.
The auction is scheduled for exactly 30 days after the will's contest window closes, which is day 1 of episode 1.
Desmond has independently inspected the basement and found its real structural condition sound, contradicting the report.

threads.label: The Frame-Up
threads.solution: Twelve years before episode 1, a fire gutted the Amaranth's east wing. Walter Tanaka and Lila's husband Henry Chen — both facing bankruptcy after Walter secretly embezzled from the building's renovation fund to cover Henry's gambling debts — staged the fire for the insurance payout. Tommy Lau, seventeen at the time, was framed with a planted gas can and a coerced statement from a paid-off security guard; he served twelve years of a fifteen-year sentence for an arson he didn't commit. Building superintendent Augustine Kwok had removed Walter's original embezzlement ledger from his office two nights before the fire, suspecting something was wrong, and has hidden it in the basement archive ever since, unable to prove foul play until Henry's death.
objective_facts: The east-wing fire occurred 12 years before episode 1's present day.
Walter Tanaka embezzled $180,000 from the building's 1998 renovation fund to cover Henry Chen's gambling debts.
Walter and Henry jointly arranged the arson for the insurance payout, timed six weeks before the embezzlement was due to be audited.
The security guard who testified against Tommy, Ray Odell, was paid $5,000 by Walter and has since left the state.
Augustine removed the original embezzlement ledger from Walter's office two nights before the fire and has hidden it since in a fireproof box behind the loose brick in the basement archive.
Henry Chen died 4 years ago without ever telling Lila the truth.
Tommy was released on parole 3 days before episode 1 begins, having served 12 of his 15-year sentence.

threads.label: The Ninth Month
threads.solution: Nora is six months pregnant by Julian Reyes, Iris's fiancé, conceived during a location-scouting trip for the auction before Iris had fully returned to the city. Julian does not know Nora is pregnant or that she intends to keep the baby. Nora spent one night with Tommy shortly after his release — unrelated to the pregnancy, since she was already carrying by then — and has let the neighborhood's assumption that Tommy is the father stand uncorrected, both to protect Julian's engagement to Iris and because Tommy has never pushed her to deny it.
objective_facts: Nora is 26 weeks pregnant as of episode 1.
Conception occurred during a dated location-scouting trip Julian took for the Amaranth auction, roughly six weeks before Iris permanently returned to the city.
Julian does not know Nora is pregnant.
Nora and Tommy spent one night together 2 days after his release, after Nora was already pregnant.
Nora has never confirmed or denied to anyone that Tommy is the father.
Nora has an ultrasound appointment scheduled for a fixed date within the season, which she has told no one about.

threads.label: The Reckoning
threads.solution: Augustine was diagnosed with terminal pancreatic cancer six weeks before episode 1 and given a prognosis of three to four months; he has told no one, including Lila. He has visited the basement archive alone at least once a week since Tommy's release, each time deciding not to hand over the ledger that would clear Tommy's name — because doing so would also destroy Lila's memory of Henry as the husband she still grieves rather than the man who let an innocent teenager take his fall.
objective_facts: Augustine was diagnosed 6 weeks before episode 1's present day.
His oncologist gave a prognosis of 3 to 4 months.
Augustine has told no one about the diagnosis.
The ledger proving Henry and Walter's fraud is in a fireproof box behind a specific loose brick in the basement archive.
Augustine has visited the basement archive alone at least once a week since Tommy's release, each time leaving the ledger untouched.
Augustine's next oncology follow-up appointment is scheduled for a fixed date within the season.

episodes.theme: Tommy's return sets the Ninth Month's central lie quietly into motion as Nora lets the neighborhood's assumption about the baby's father stand, while Augustine's routine, unremarked visit to the archive recommits the Frame-Up's secret to silence for another night.
episodes.outro_note: In her mail, unopened, sits the structural engineer's report Iris hasn't yet seen — and thirty days start counting the moment she signs it. Two floors below, Augustine folds a hospital reminder into his pocket before anyone can ask what it says.

sections.title: Congratulations, I Guess
sections.what: Nora arrives visibly pregnant; Desmond congratulates her and Tommy as a couple, assuming he's the father, and Nora lets the assumption stand uncorrected.

sections.title: Henry's Booth
sections.what: Lila welcomes Tommy home with a free meal in Henry's old booth; her grief for her husband surfaces as Tommy's unspoken bitterness about his conviction lingers.

sections.title: Bad Water Pressure
sections.what: Alone in the greenhouse, Augustine waters plants through a private stab of pain he hides, quietly calculating how much longer he can keep his diagnosis secret.

sections.title: What the Numbers Say

sections.title: The Loose Brick
sections.what: Alone in the basement on the night of Tommy's release, Augustine checks the fireproof box behind the loose brick and again leaves the ledger hidden.

sections.title: Three Days, One Night
sections.what: Nora finds Tommy alone on the rooftop at night; their unfinished history from two nights together resurfaces, though neither one names the baby aloud.

case.label: The Frame-Up

character_knowledge.TOMMY_LAU: Tommy knows he's innocent of the arson and served twelve years for it, and he's returning to the exact building he was convicted of trying to burn down. He genuinely doesn't know who framed him or why, and wrongly believes Ray Odell's testimony against him was simple neighborhood malice rather than a $5,000 payoff, since he never learned the guard was compensated. He's guarded about how much anger he lets show, especially around Desmond, who was kind to him before and after prison.
character_knowledge.DESMOND_HALE: Desmond has managed the Amaranth for eighteen years and remembers the fire well enough to privately doubt Tommy ever set it, though he has no evidence and has never said so aloud. He welcomes Tommy back without judgment, genuinely uncertain what actually happened that night, and unaware of Walter and Henry's role or Augustine's hidden ledger.
character_knowledge.LILA_CHEN: Lila still grieves Henry as a good man who kept the building's old books and never told her anything was wrong; she has no idea he helped stage the fire or embezzle from the renovation fund. She privately doubts Tommy's guilt, having known him as a boy, but has never voiced that doubt because it would mean questioning Henry, which she isn't ready to do.
character_knowledge.AUGUSTINE_KWOK: Augustine alone knows the full truth: Walter and Henry staged the fire, framed Tommy with a planted gas can, and paid off Ray Odell, and he has kept the original embezzlement ledger hidden behind a loose brick in the basement archive ever since. Tonight, as every week since Tommy's release, he visits the archive and again chooses not to retrieve it, protecting Lila's memory of Henry rather than clearing Tommy's name.

episodes.theme: Iris locks the Amaranth's fate to a fabricated report she trusts completely, formalizing the Auction Clock's countdown, while Augustine's small, practiced act of hiding a hospital reminder shows the Reckoning's secret is already a habit, not a slip.
episodes.intro_note: Three days since Tommy Lau came home, and already the building has settled back into old habits: Nora let Desmond's assumption about her baby's father stand unspoken in the lobby, and Augustine climbed down to the basement archive one more time, leaving whatever he keeps there undisturbed. Now, as the auction clock ticks toward its thirtieth day, the Amaranth's residents move through their routines, unaware of how many of those routines are quietly rehearsed lies.
episodes.outro_note: The auction is locked, the paperwork signed, and somewhere behind seed packets in the greenhouse, another envelope waits with the others no one has found. Thirty days now separate the Amaranth from an ending no one in the building has yet imagined, and for one more week, at least, the building's routines will hold.

episodes.theme: A quieter episode of ordinary building life — Desmond's rounds, Lila's diner rhythms, Augustine's greenhouse routines — that lets every established secret sit undisturbed while the season's relationships breathe before the complications ahead.
episodes.intro_note: Three days after Iris signed the auction into motion, the building exhales. The countdown keeps running underneath the floorboards, but for one day, nothing forces itself to the surface.
episodes.outro_note: The quiet won't hold for long. Tomorrow, a shelf in the basement archive and a booth in the diner will each come within inches of giving something away.

sections.what: Desmond walks Tommy through routine building fixes and small favors, easing him back into daily rhythms without dwelling on his twelve years away.

sections.title: The Basement Wall

sections.title: Free Coffee

sections.title: Slower Than Usual

sections.title: The Bad Back

sections.title: By the Mailboxes

sections.title: Among the Files

sections.title: The Math

episodes.outro_note: Lila's question about her husband's handwriting is loose in the building now, and so is the strain it's put on Augustine — and two more people are about to start noticing what he's carrying: one in a briefcase full of paperwork, the other in an empty basement doorway.

sections.title: The Numbers Don't Match
sections.what: Late at night, Lila cross-checks Henry's handwritten ledgers against the diner's books and finds numbers from 1998 that don't match anything of her own.

sections.title: Confronting Augustine
sections.what: Lila brings the ledger page to Augustine in the basement archive, demanding what he knows about Walter's 1998 renovation fund; he denies knowing more.

sections.title: Steadying Himself
sections.what: Watering the greenhouse alone, Augustine steadies himself through a sudden wave of pain, calming his breathing just before Nora arrives to ask after him.

sections.title: Unanswered
sections.what: In the lobby, Nora and Tommy pass each other; his question about the baby still unanswered hangs between them as she deflects with small talk.

sections.title: Trusting the Paperwork
sections.what: In the diner, Desmond presses Iris again about the structural report's inconsistencies, but she insists the numbers are solid, trusting Julian's paperwork completely.

sections.title: Rattled
sections.what: Rattled, Lila corners Desmond in the lobby and admits something about Henry's old books feels wrong, without naming what, then refuses his help.

sections.title: Searching Again
sections.what: Tommy returns to the basement archive searching for his old case file, unaware Lila has just found a ledger discrepancy tied to the same building records.

sections.title: Not Answering
sections.what: In the archive after Lila leaves, Augustine lingers by the loose brick and doesn't answer when Desmond calls down the stairs checking he's alright.

episodes.intro_note: A few days after Lila found Henry's name tangled in Walter's old renovation numbers—and after Augustine's flat denial that he knew anything more—the building has gone quiet on the surface, though nothing she read has stopped turning over in her mind.
episodes.outro_note: Desmond has a lie he can't yet explain and Iris has papers she can't yet name, and neither one has noticed that Nora's own calendar has a single date circled that she hasn't shown to anyone at the Amaranth.

sections.title: The Missing Tuesday
sections.what: Desmond tells Lila he has missed Augustine at their usual Tuesday coffee twice running now and admits he's starting to worry.

sections.title: The Open Briefcase
sections.what: Iris waits alone while Julian steps outside to take a call, and glancing into his open briefcase finds bank records tying the LLC's debt purchases to one shared account.

sections.title: A Bad Knee
sections.what: Augustine waters the greenhouse beside Nora and brushes off a wince of pain as a bad knee, the same lie he's used before.

sections.title: The Hardware Store
sections.what: Desmond intercepts Augustine returning to the lobby and asks where he spent the afternoon; Augustine claims the hardware store, but Desmond has spotted a clinic parking stub in his coat.

sections.title: The Shared Account
sections.what: Iris shows Desmond the account records from Julian's briefcase, and his own suspicions about the fabricated inspection report suddenly feel less separate from hers.

sections.title: The Bulb Still Burns
sections.what: Shaken after Desmond's questions, Augustine retreats to the basement archive and sits near the loose brick for a long time without touching it.

sections.title: Henry's Ghost
sections.what: Tommy notices Lila's distraction at the diner and asks what's wrong; she deflects toward the auction instead of the discovery she made in Henry's old books.

sections.title: Two Tuesdays
sections.what: Alone in the lobby beneath the stopped clock, Iris studies the photocopied records, weighing whether to confront Julian directly, and pockets them instead.

episodes.theme: Nora keeps her ultrasound appointment entirely alone, the Ninth Month's countdown reaching its most private milestone yet without resolving anything about who the father is or will be.
episodes.intro_note: Two Tuesdays missed, and Desmond's question still hangs in the basement air — Augustine gave him nothing but a thin excuse. Upstairs, Iris hasn't let go of the numbers she found in Julian's briefcase, not yet sure what they add up to. Today, though, the building's attention turns to someone who slipped out before breakfast, telling no one where.
episodes.outro_note: Three days now stand between the Amaranth and the auction block. In the basement, a loose brick still waits behind Augustine's silence. In a diner booth, Lila's question about her husband's handwriting hasn't gone away. And in a greenhouse gone quiet for the night, Nora holds a black-and-white photograph no one else at the Amaranth has seen — the season's clocks are all running down at once, and only one of them has just quietly, privately, ticked forward.

sections.title: Alone in the Greenhouse

sections.title: Circling the Ledger
sections.what: Lila lays Henry's discrepancy on the counter again and watches Augustine's hands go still; he denies knowing more, and she almost, almost believes him.

sections.title: Back Before Dark
sections.what: Nora slides into her usual booth that evening; Lila notices she's quieter than her jokes let on, and Nora talks fast past every question.

sections.title: Three Days Out
sections.what: With the auction three days off, Desmond tells Iris his own basement inspection still contradicts the report, and for the first time, she hesitates.

sections.what: Narrator marks day thirty as Desmond sets folding chairs in the lobby; Iris arrives resolved to sell, still unaware Julian intends to be the buyer.

sections.title: The Loose Brick
sections.what: Augustine finds Tommy searching old files again and, without explaining why, pries the loose brick free himself and pockets the fireproof box.

sections.title: What the Ledger Says
sections.what: With Lila and Tommy in the basement, Augustine opens the ledger and reads aloud how Walter, Henry, and paid witness Ray Odell built Tommy's frame.

sections.title: Six Weeks
sections.what: Alone with Lila after Tommy leaves to absorb the news, Augustine tells her he has terminal cancer, diagnosed six weeks ago and hidden since.

sections.title: The Numbered LLC
sections.what: Moments before the gavel falls, Iris takes the floor and publicly names Julian as the numbered LLC's controlling signatory behind the auction.

sections.title: After the Gavel
sections.what: In the stunned lobby, Desmond confirms his own basement inspection was sound all along, while Iris grasps how long Julian had been lying.

sections.title: Whose Clock Now
sections.what: In the greenhouse, Nora tells Tommy she went to the ultrasound alone and still won't say whose child it is, leaving him unanswered.

sections.title: What Doesn't Close
sections.what: At the diner past midnight, Lila sits with the ledger's truth and Augustine's diagnosis both raw, unsure which loss she's grieving first.

case.label: The Auction Clock

character_knowledge.IRIS_TANAKA: She has spent days quietly tracing the numbered LLC's debt purchases to a shared account without confirming the name behind it; standing at the auction, the last piece falls into place in real time and she names Julian publicly before she has fully absorbed the personal betrayal herself.
character_knowledge.DESMOND_HALE: He has known since his own basement inspection that the structural report was fabricated and has said so without ever learning who paid for it; Iris's public exposure of Julian confirms an instinct he's carried for episodes but could never prove alone.

case.solution: Walter Tanaka died eight months before episode 1, leaving the Amaranth split fifty-fifty between his daughter Iris and a trust whose controlling signatory — hidden behind a numbered LLC — is Julian Reyes, Iris's fiancé. For two years Julian has quietly bought up the building's deferred maintenance debt and paid a structural engineer to fabricate the worst findings in a damning inspection report, engineering a thirty-day forced auction that Iris genuinely believes is her own grief-driven decision, unaware Julian intends to be its winning bidder.
objective_facts: Walter Tanaka died 8 months before episode 1, leaving the Amaranth split 50/50 between Iris and a trust.
The trust's controlling signatory is Julian Reyes, though its public filings list only a numbered LLC.
Julian has purchased $340,000 of the building's deferred maintenance debt over the past two years without Iris's knowledge.
The structural engineer's report cites two fabricated defects that Julian paid the inspector to add.
The auction is scheduled for exactly 30 days after the will's contest window closes, which is day 1 of episode 1.
Desmond has independently inspected the basement and found its real structural condition sound, contradicting the report.

case.label: The Frame-Up

character_knowledge.AUGUSTINE_KWOK: He has hidden the embezzlement ledger for twelve years to protect Lila's memory of Henry, but between Tommy's persistent digging and his own narrowing timeline he decides tonight the truth outweighs that protection, and reads it aloud himself rather than let it be found by accident.
character_knowledge.TOMMY_LAU: He has only ever known he was framed, not by whom or how; hearing the ledger read aloud gives him his first real confirmation of Walter and Henry's scheme and Ray Odell's paid testimony, though he learns nothing here about Julian or Nora's pregnancy.
character_knowledge.LILA_CHEN: She arrived suspecting only that Henry's old books hid some financial irregularity tied to Walter's renovation fund; this scene is the first time she learns the full extent — that her husband helped orchestrate an innocent teenager's twelve-year imprisonment — and she has no way to soften it.

case.solution: Twelve years before episode 1, a fire gutted the Amaranth's east wing. Walter Tanaka and Lila's husband Henry Chen — both facing bankruptcy after Walter secretly embezzled from the building's renovation fund to cover Henry's gambling debts — staged the fire for the insurance payout. Tommy Lau, seventeen at the time, was framed with a planted gas can and a coerced statement from a paid-off security guard; he served twelve years of a fifteen-year sentence for an arson he didn't commit. Building superintendent Augustine Kwok had removed Walter's original embezzlement ledger from his office two nights before the fire, suspecting something was wrong, and has hidden it in the basement archive ever since, unable to prove foul play until Henry's death.
objective_facts: The east-wing fire occurred 12 years before episode 1's present day.
Walter Tanaka embezzled $180,000 from the building's 1998 renovation fund to cover Henry Chen's gambling debts.
Walter and Henry jointly arranged the arson for the insurance payout, timed six weeks before the embezzlement was due to be audited.
The security guard who testified against Tommy, Ray Odell, was paid $5,000 by Walter and has since left the state.
Augustine removed the original embezzlement ledger from Walter's office two nights before the fire and has hidden it since in a fireproof box behind the loose brick in the basement archive.
Henry Chen died 4 years ago without ever telling Lila the truth.
Tommy was released on parole 3 days before episode 1 begins, having served 12 of his 15-year sentence.

case.label: The Reckoning

character_knowledge.AUGUSTINE_KWOK: He has told no one about his diagnosis for six weeks, including Lila; having just chosen to break one silence with the ledger, he decides in the same breath not to leave this second truth unspoken, and tells her plainly that he is dying.
character_knowledge.LILA_CHEN: She had noticed Augustine tiring and slowing lately but assumed it was age or the greenhouse's demands, not illness; his confession lands as a second, separate blow in the same conversation where she is still absorbing what Henry did.

case.solution: Augustine was diagnosed with terminal pancreatic cancer six weeks before episode 1 and given a prognosis of three to four months; he has told no one, including Lila. He has visited the basement archive alone at least once a week since Tommy's release, each time deciding not to hand over the ledger that would clear Tommy's name — because doing so would also destroy Lila's memory of Henry as the husband she still grieves rather than the man who let an innocent teenager take his fall.
objective_facts: Augustine was diagnosed 6 weeks before episode 1's present day.
His oncologist gave a prognosis of 3 to 4 months.
Augustine has told no one about the diagnosis.
The ledger proving Henry and Walter's fraud is in a fireproof box behind a specific loose brick in the basement archive.
Augustine has visited the basement archive alone at least once a week since Tommy's release, each time leaving the ledger untouched.
Augustine's next oncology follow-up appointment is scheduled for a fixed date within the season.

case.label: The Ninth Month

character_knowledge.NORA_VANCE: She alone knows what the ultrasound showed and still hasn't named the father to anyone; she deflects Tommy's question again, aware the neighborhood's assumption about him is protecting Julian's engagement, though she senses that protection can't hold much longer.
character_knowledge.TOMMY_LAU: He still doesn't know whether the baby is his; freshly confronted with the truth about his own frame-up, he pushes Nora once more for a direct answer about the child, and leaves the greenhouse without getting one.

case.solution: Nora is six months pregnant by Julian Reyes, Iris's fiancé, conceived during a location-scouting trip for the auction before Iris had fully returned to the city. Julian does not know Nora is pregnant or that she intends to keep the baby. Nora spent one night with Tommy shortly after his release — unrelated to the pregnancy, since she was already carrying by then — and has let the neighborhood's assumption that Tommy is the father stand uncorrected, both to protect Julian's engagement to Iris and because Tommy has never pushed her to deny it.
objective_facts: Nora is 26 weeks pregnant as of episode 1.
Conception occurred during a dated location-scouting trip Julian took for the Amaranth auction, roughly six weeks before Iris permanently returned to the city.
Julian does not know Nora is pregnant.
Nora and Tommy spent one night together 2 days after his release, after Nora was already pregnant.
Nora has never confirmed or denied to anyone that Tommy is the father.
Nora has an ultrasound appointment scheduled for a fixed date within the season, which she has told no one about.
